title: Health associate professionals n.e.c.
description: Promote health within a community by assisting individuals to adopt healthy behaviors. Serve as an advocate for the health needs of individuals by assisting community residents in effectively communicating with healthcare providers or social service agencies. Act as liaison or advocate and implement programs that promote, maintain, and improve individual and overall community health. May deliver health-related preventive services such as blood pressure, glaucoma, and hearing screenings. May collect data to help identify community health needs. Assess, treat, and care for patients with breathing disorders. Assume primary responsibility for all respiratory care modalities, including the supervision of respiratory therapy technicians. Initiate and conduct therapeutic procedures; maintain patient records; and select, assemble, check, and operate equipment. Conduct tests on pulmonary or cardiovascular systems of patients for diagnostic, therapeutic, or research purposes. May conduct or assist in electrocardiograms, cardiac catheterizations, pulmonary functions, lung capacity, and similar tests. (Based on 21 related US occupations.)

tasks.task: Identify and contact members of high-risk or targeted groups, such as minority and low-income populations or pregnant women, and advocate for their health needs with government agencies or health service providers. Advise clients or community groups on issues related to risk prevention, sanitation, self-care, and social development to promote overall health.

Attend community meetings or health fairs to build relationships with community members and develop plans to improve overall health. Conduct home visits for pregnant women, newborns, or other high-risk individuals to monitor their progress or assess their needs. Collect information from individuals to compile vital statistics about the general health of the community.

Provide basic health services, including first aid and perform routine health-related preventive services such as blood pressure screening and diagnostic tests, while advising on issues related to improving general health through diet, exercise, and immunization.

Transport or accompany clients to scheduled health appointments, and refer community members to necessary health services. Engage in communication and coordination with patients, family members, and medical staff involved in their care.

Collect and report data on community health needs and develop outreach materials such as newsletters and brochures to disseminate health information to community members. Educate individuals and groups about health matters and assist them in accessing appropriate services.

Maintain updated client records and document relevant information, while ensuring accessibility and continuity of care. Monitor clients' progress and communicate updates to other healthcare professionals.
Advise clients or community groups on social services applications and assist families with accessing programs designed to improve health and well-being, such as Medicaid and WIC.

Engage in research or continuous education to keep abreast of developments in the health field and improve service delivery. Participate in interdisciplinary meetings to discuss and enhance patient care.
Perform emergency care as needed, including basic life support and CPR, to ensure immediate health needs are addressed.
Train or instruct new employees or community members on health-related procedures, ensuring a knowledgeable and competent approach to community health advocacy.
Calibrate, troubleshoot, or repair equipment and correct malfunctions as needed.
Summarize technical data to assist physicians in diagnosing brain, sleep, or nervous system disorders.
Assist educational specialists or clinical psychologists in administering situational or diagnostic tests to measure clients' abilities or progress.
Perform clerical duties, such as scheduling appointments, collecting data, or documenting health insurance billings.
Maintain and promote a positive attitude toward clients and their treatment programs.
Monitor patients' performance in therapy activities, providing encouragement, and select therapy activities to fit their needs and capabilities.
Instruct or assist in instructing patients and families in home programs, basic living skills, or the care and use of adaptive equipment.
Evaluate the daily living skills or capacities of clients with physical, developmental, or mental health disabilities.
Aid patients in dressing and grooming themselves, and implement treatment plans designed to help clients function independently.
Report to supervisors, verbally or in writing, on patients' progress, attitudes, and behavior; alter treatment programs if they are not having the intended effect.

Work under the direction of occupational therapists to plan, implement, or administer educational, vocational, or recreational programs that restore or enhance performance in individuals with functional impairments, and design, fabricate, or repair assistive devices or make adaptive changes to equipment or environments.
Assemble, clean, or maintain equipment or materials for patient use, supervise or train other therapy staff, and maintain proper sterilization techniques as needed.

Teach patients how to deal constructively with their emotions and collaborate with or assist doctors, psychologists, or rehabilitation therapists in working with patients with cognitive, intellectual, or developmental disabilities to treat and rehabilitate them.
Transport patients to and from therapy areas and observe and record patients' progress, attitudes, and behavior while maintaining client records.
Monitor patients' vital signs, such as blood pressure and heart rate, during therapeutic procedures and explain testing or treatment procedures to patients to gain cooperation and reduce anxiety.
Conduct tests to evaluate patients' cardiopulmonary functions and monitor patients' physical and emotional well-being, reporting any unusual behavior or physical ailments to medical staff.
Ensure careful adherence to physicians' orders and monitor patients' physiological responses to therapy, consulting with physicians if adverse reactions occur.
Maintain charts that contain patients' pertinent identification and therapy information, and relay blood analysis results to a physician.
Educate patients and their families about their conditions and teach appropriate disease management techniques, such as breathing exercises or the use of medications or respiratory equipment.

Inspect, clean, test, and maintain respiratory therapy equipment to ensure it is functioning safely and efficiently, ordering repairs when necessary, and monitor patients' responses during therapy sessions.
Provide emergency first aid to sick or injured animals, if applicable within the scope of work activities.
Clean and maintain kennels, examination or treatment areas to control the spread of disease, not relevant to the primary role of health associate professionals.
Gather, arrange, or assemble instruments or supplies, maintaining a proper sterile field during procedures and obtaining necessary equipment for operative procedures.
Prepare patients for surgery, including positioning them and covering them with sterile drapes; coordinate with anesthesia personnel to maintain patient temperature.
Assist with patient resuscitation during cardiac arrest or other life-threatening events and monitor patient intra-operative status, such as vital signs or blood loss.
Monitor and maintain aseptic technique throughout procedures and maintain an unobstructed operative field, using surgical retractors, sponges, or suctioning and irrigating equipment.
Perform hygiene-related duties and assist in applying casts, splints, braces, or similar devices as required.
Develop or teach strategies to promote client wellness and independence, and encourage patients to develop work skills and participate in social, recreational, or therapeutic activities.
Lead prescribed individual or group therapy sessions as part of specific therapeutic procedures.

Assess skin integrity and other body conditions upon completion of procedures to determine if damage has occurred from body positioning. Maintain documentation of all patients' contacts, reviewing and updating records as necessary. Obtain complete health and medical histories from patients including medical, surgical, reproductive, and mental health histories. Monitor maternal condition during labor by checking vital signs, monitoring uterine contractions, or performing physical examinations.

Test patients' hemoglobin, hematocrit, and blood glucose levels; collect specimens from patients using standard medical procedures. Conduct ongoing prenatal health assessments, tracking changes in physical and emotional health. Monitor fetal growth and well-being through heartbeat detection and body measurement.

Provide necessary medical care for infants at birth, including emergency care such as resuscitation. Perform post-partum health assessments of mothers and babies at regular intervals. Provide information about the physical and emotional processes involved in pregnancy, labor, birth, and postpartum periods.

Develop, implement, or evaluate individualized plans for midwifery care. Establish and follow emergency or contingency plans for mothers and newborns. Identify, monitor, or treat pregnancy-related problems such as hypertension, gestational diabetes, pre-term labor, or retarded fetal growth.

Provide, or refer patients to other providers for, education or counseling on topics such as genetic testing, newborn care, contraception, or breastfeeding. Counsel women regarding the nutritional requirements of pregnancy. Provide comfort and relaxation measures for mothers in labor through interventions such as massage or breathing techniques.

Conduct in-service training sessions, or family and community education programs. Collaborate in research studies. Incorporate research findings into practice as appropriate. Complete birth certificates and compile and evaluate clinical practice statistics.

Assist physicians or registered nurses in the conduct of endoscopic procedures. Select or prepare speech-language instructional materials and assist speech-language pathologists in various functions. Document clients' progress toward meeting established treatment objectives and maintain inventories of endoscopic equipment and supplies.

Evaluate patients' laboratory and medical records, requesting assistance from other practitioners when necessary. Identify tubal and ectopic pregnancies and refer patients for treatments. Assess birthing environments to ensure cleanliness, safety, and the availability of appropriate supplies.

emerging_tasks.task: Attend high-risk and caesarian section infant deliveries to provide neonatal respiratory care as needed.
Perform general administrative tasks, such as answering telephones, scheduling appointments, or ordering supplies or equipment.
Transport patients to and from the operating room.
Test patients for sexually transmitted infections.
